metadata.purpose: Red/yellow bolete complexes, red-pored caution boletes, and strongly staining allies.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Shared warning-group record for red/orange-pored boletes and red/yellow boletes that bruise rapidly or strongly blue. This is a caution anchor, not a food target.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Treat red/orange pores plus rapid or strong blue staining as a stop sign unless a qualified expert confirms the identification.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food. Red/orange-pored boletes and red/yellow boletes that bruise rapidly or strongly blue are difficult to separate; some can cause significant gastrointestinal illness, and beginner separation is unreliable.
records.general_notes: This is a safety grouping, not a single species. It catches the red/orange-pored and strong blue-staining bolete lane so users do not accidentally treat all boletes as edible.

stem_feature: Often reticulate or dotted; variable by species
smell: Variable; avoid if unpleasant or uncertain
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Varies by species; many members produce olive to olive-brown deposits. This is broad warning-group guidance only.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly summer into fall; treat as caution whenever found.

edible_use.notes: Avoid for food. Several red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes can cause significant gastrointestinal illness, and beginner separation is unreliable.

records.scientific_name: Red/orange-pored and strong blue-staining bolete warning group; includes bicolor-like, sensibilis/pseudosensibilis-type, red-mouth/lurid, and similar caution boletes as field context.
records.field_identification: Umbrella caution group for boletes with red to orange pore surfaces and/or rapid, strong blue staining. Check pore color, bruising speed, flesh color change, stem reticulation or dots, cap color, smell, and tree association. In this guide, red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes are not food targets.
records.mushroom_card_note: Red/orange pores or strong blue staining = caution lane. Do not eat from this group unless expert-confirmed.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Key to red-capped, blue-staining boletes
use_links.notes: Explains why red-capped / blue-staining bolete IDs are difficult and often require advanced work.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus bicolor
use_links.notes: Useful comparison for true bicolor traits versus stronger blue-staining/red-pored confusion.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus page six
use_links.notes: Keys eastern blue-staining boletes and related confusing taxa.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus sensibilis
use_links.notes: Representative red/yellow pore, blue-staining caution bolete.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus pseudosensibilis
use_links.notes: Representative red/yellow pore, blue-staining caution comparison.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Frost's bolete
use_links.notes: Representative red-pored bolete used for caution-group comparison.

records.quality_notes: Representative caution group, not one species. Images and notes show mixed examples of red-pored and/or blue-staining risk patterns.
records.eating_use_notes: No food use recommended in this guide.
records.risk_reason: Many red-capped, red-pored, and blue-staining boletes are hard to separate; some are poisonous or not recommended, and names/concepts have shifted.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weakness, and dehydration.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Red/orange pore surfaces, rapid blue staining, mixed red/yellow bolete color patterns, and confusing online/field-guide naming.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat red/orange-pored or fast/strong blue-staining boletes unless keyed by an expert.
records.caution_reason: Shared caution group; red/orange pores and fast strong blue staining are stop signs for this app.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds only broad group-level spore guidance; it does not clear species-level identification, review debt, or food use.

records.culinary_uses: Not a casual food target in this guide. Some true two-colored boletes are eaten by experienced foragers, but red/yellow/blue-staining bolete confusion risk is high; cook only expert-confirmed young specimens.
records.notes: Red-and-yellow bolete group with blue-bruising look-alike risk; keep the shared red-pored/blue-staining warning context visible.

mushroom_profile.summary: Two-colored Bolete group: red-and-yellow bolete with shallow yellow pores and blue-bruising comparison risk; experienced-only and not a casual food target.
staining: Pores bruise blue promptly; flesh usually faint/erratic blue
stem_feature: Red stem with yellow near apex
pore_color: Bright yellow when young; bruising blue

records.edibility_detail: Experienced-only. Do not eat red/orange-pored or fast/strongly blue-staining boletes unless expert-confirmed; use only confidently identified true bicolor-type specimens and cook thoroughly.
records.field_identification: Red cap and stem with bright yellow pores when young, shallow tube layer, and limited/appropriate blue bruising for true bicolor. Reject red/orange-pored or fast/strongly blue-staining boletes unless expert-confirmed.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus bicolor
use_links.notes: Red/yellow color pattern, shallow tubes, and blue bruising comparison.

records.look_alike_notes: Compare with the shared red-pored / blue-staining bolete warning group plus named caution examples such as red-mouth and lurid boletes. Boletus sensibilis / pseudosensibilis-type names remain useful comparison context; this record now routes the broader warning through the shared caution-group card.
records.edibility_notes: Some two-colored boletes are eaten, but this group is easy to confuse with faster-bluing or more suspect red/yellow boletes.

edible_use.notes: Cook only confidently identified true bicolor-type specimens. Do not eat red/orange-pored or fast/strongly blue-staining boletes unless expert-confirmed.

records.foraging_value: Experienced-only caution; not a casual food target
records.quality_notes: Good table value only when the bicolor bolete trait set is nailed down; not a beginner bolete.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed young specimens; do not use if ID drifts toward fast-bluing or red/yellow caution boletes.
records.risk_reason: Risk is confusion with Boletus sensibilis, B. pseudosensibilis, and other red/yellow blue-staining boletes.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat unless true bicolor traits are confirmed: red cap/stem, bright yellow young pores, shallow tubes, and flesh that blues faintly or erratically rather than instantly everywhere.

lookalike_risk.label: High — expert bolete ID required
lookalike_risk.summary: Bicolor-like red/yellow boletes require expert-level separation from red-pored and fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Confirm red cap/stem with bright yellow young pores
Reject red/orange pores, very rapid dark-blue staining, extensive blue flesh staining, or bitter taste
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Red- or orange-pored boletes, very rapid dark-blue staining, or extensive blue staining through the flesh mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous or suspect red/yellow bolete confusion.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not eat bicolor-like boletes unless true bicolor traits are confirmed.

records.edibility_detail: Do not eat red/yellow blue-staining boletes from this record without specialist confirmation.
records.mushroom_card_note: Rare bicolor-like red/yellow bolete recorded from Michigan sources; treat as a specialist ID with low local confidence rather than a food target.
records.field_identification: Use only as a specialist comparison for a red-stemmed Michigan bolete with unusually long, strongly amyloid spores. Do not infer Upper Michigan occurrence or identify from color alone.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Red/yellow blue-staining bolete in a hard-to-separate caution group; field confidence is not high enough for food use.

mushroom_profile.summary: Brown-pored oak bolete that bruises blue/black; the whole vermiculosus group needs cautious, modern treatment.
cap_surface: Olive brown to medium brown cap
pore_color: Dark red-brown to maroon young pores

records.general_notes: Part of a difficult Brown-pored Bolete group rather than a simple duplicate. Treat species-level ID as tentative without expert review; compare cap color, pore color, staining, stem surface, and associated trees. This record represents lighter brown/yellowish cap tendency and smaller-spored concept reported in field-guide sources.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food; retained for ID/taxonomy comparison.
records.field_identification: Oak-associated bolete with dark reddish-brown to maroon young pores fading dull orange-brown, non-reticulate stem, and blue-to-black bruising. Often wormy and taxonomically tricky.
records.mushroom_card_note: Brown-pored oak bolete that bruises blue/black; the whole vermiculosus group needs cautious, modern treatment.
records.non_edible_severity: Caution / not a beginner food target

taste: Bitter (expert/reference trait only; do not taste unknown boletes)
stem_feature: Pink to red streaks or reticulation

mushroom_profile.summary: Candy Apple Bolete: sticky bright red cap, red pores, coarsely reticulate red/yellow stem, and prompt blue bruising.

records.edibility_detail: Use only with expert-level confidence; not a beginner food mushroom.
records.field_identification: Look for a truly bright red sticky cap, red pore surface that blues, a deeply/coarsely reticulate stem, hardwood/oak association, and summer/fall timing.
records.edibility_notes: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.mushroom_card_note: Candy Apple Bolete caution/review record suppressed from normal cards until image-rights metadata is completed; red-pored bolete caution applies.
records.source_note: Visible image-rights/status debt removed from normal cards in v4.3.149 by suppressing the record until Exsudoporus frostii images are fully rights-cleaned. v4.3.210 adds a limited group-level spore-print description and normalized filters; finer separation remains review-sensitive and may require a thick print or microscopy.
records.suppression_reason: Image-rights metadata is incomplete for the visible Commons photo set; hidden from normal cards until the Exsudoporus frostii images are rights-cleaned.

records.culinary_uses: Do not collect for meals in this guide.

records.non_edible_severity: Avoid — red/yellow bolete with strong instant blue bruising
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Red to orangish-red cap, yellow to orangish stem with reddish areas, yellow pore surface aging dingy orangish-brown, and very strong/instant dark blue bruising on handling or slicing.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus sensibilis
use_links.notes: Instant blue bruising and comparison with bicolor-like boletes.

records.edibility_notes: Fast, strong blue bruising in a red/yellow bolete is a stop sign for this app. Keep it as a caution/look-alike record, not a meal record.

records.taxonomy_note: True Suillellus luridus is European. North American look-alikes remain unresolved and are represented here only as a comparison group.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Red Crack Bolete.
staining: Blue staining in cut or bruised flesh
mushroom_profile.season_note: Early summer into late fall / mid-winter in some broad references; for Upper Michigan treat as summer-fall pending local confirmation.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.field_identification: Small pore-bearing bolete with a brownish dry/tomentose cap that often cracks to reveal reddish flesh below the cap skin. Yellow angular pores, olive-brown spore print, yellow stem with coral-red fibrils or red lower-stem tones, and blue staining in cut flesh help place it, but this species complex is often hard to separate without microscopy.

field_measurements.cap: Cap about 4–10 cm wide / about 1.5–4 in wide
field_measurements.stem: Stem about 4–13 cm long and 1–2 cm thick / about 1.6–5.1 in long and 0.4–0.8 in thick
field_measurements.notes: Several similar Xerocomellus/Hortiboletus taxa overlap; measurements support field sorting but do not prove species-level ID alone.

records.edibility_detail: Often listed as edible but not choice; do not rely on this group for food because similar cracked/red/yellow boletes are difficult and older specimens decay quickly.

images_structured.rights_notes: Exact rights metadata copied from the repo's render-credits.js entry (credits-enrichment-v73); no image is used unless file-level author/license data are available.

completion_gaps: Only one exact-rights image verified in the local credit map; target photo coverage is 2–3 distinct images.
Species complex can require microscopy or expert confirmation; keep as caution/incomplete rather than a beginner-complete edible.

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.source_note: Stale placeholder-image note removed because a real image is already present.

records.culinary_uses: Do not collect for meals in this guide.
records.notes: Red/orange-pored bolete group with strong blue staining. Treat as a caution group in this guide; the red pore surface and fast bruising are the field marks that matter.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Red Mouth Bolete.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Olive when a usable deposit is obtained. Prints may be weak or absent in some collections; keep the group-level warning posture.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer, especially June through September in hardwoods.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.general_notes: Use this entry as a warning bucket for red-pored, blue-staining boletes until individual species are confidently separated.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Brown to olive-brown cap, red to reddish-orange pores, yellow/red stem without strong reticulation, blue to blackish bruising, and velvety or fuzzy lower stem/base under hardwoods.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus subvelutipes group
use_links.notes: Red pores, blue staining, hardwood association, and fuzzy base traits.

records.edibility_notes: Brown-capped, red-pored, blue-staining boletes are a caution group. Treat as avoid unless a specific trusted species has been confirmed separately.
